parentQuestion: A 68-year-old diabetic patient steps on a tack but doesn't notice until she sees blood on the floor. She can still feel light touch on her feet with a cotton swab. Which sensory pathway has likely failed?

scaffolds.q: Diabetes can damage peripheral nerves over time (diabetic neuropathy).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! High blood sugar damages nerves, especially in the feet and hands. This is called diabetic neuropathy.

scaffolds.q: Pain and light touch are carried by different types of nerve fibers.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Pain travels on small, unmyelinated fibers (C and Aδ). Light touch travels on larger, myelinated fibers (Aβ). Different fibers, different vulnerabilities.

scaffolds.q: Small nerve fibers are more vulnerable to diabetic damage than large fibers.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Small fibers have higher metabolic demands and are damaged first. Pain and temperature sensation disappear before light touch.

scaffolds.q: Nociceptors (pain receptors) are free nerve endings that use small nerve fibers.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Pain signals travel on unmyelinated C fibers and thinly myelinated Aδ fibers. These small fibers are fragile and die first in diabetes.

scaffolds.q: Light touch receptors (like Meissner's corpuscles) use larger, more protected nerve fibers.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Light touch travels on thick, well-myelinated Aβ fibers. These survive longer in diabetic neuropathy.

scaffolds.q: A patient can lose pain sensation while keeping light touch if only the small fibers are damaged.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This is called dissociated sensory loss. Small fibers die (no pain), large fibers survive (touch preserved). Different fibers, different fates.

scaffolds.q: This diabetic patient lost her pain alarm system but kept her touch sensors.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! She can feel the cotton swab (light touch intact) but not the tack (pain lost). Small fibers died, large fibers survived.

scaffolds.q: Free nerve endings (nociceptors) have likely failed because she feels touch but not pain.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The small fibers carrying pain signals from free nerve endings are damaged. Large fibers carrying touch are intact.

scaffolds.q: The sensory pathway that has failed is carried by ___ (receptors that detect harmful stimuli).
scaffolds.explain: Free nerve endings (nociceptors)! These detect pain and travel on small, vulnerable fibers. Diabetic neuropathy killed her pain sensation while sparing touch.
